data.title: Dollar on the Blockchain: The Bigger Game Behind the GENIUS Act
data.description:
The total market capitalization of stablecoins has surpassed $320 billion, and USDT and USDC together hold more US Treasury bonds than Australia's national foreign exchange reserves. Nearly a year after the signing of the US GENIUS Act, a series of supporting regulations have been implemented. This is not just a cryptocurrency regulatory law—it represents a strategic restructuring of the dollar's hegemony in the digital age.

I. Four days ago, the BIS sounded the alarm

On April 20, 2026, Pablo Hernández de Cos, General Manager of the Bank for International Settlements (BIS), issued the strongest warning to date regarding stablecoins.

He specifically named USDT and USDC—which together account for 90% of the approximately $320 billion stablecoin market—stating that these two assets behave more like investment products than cash, and outlining five specific risks: credit supply contraction, financial instability, monetary policy failure, erosion of fiscal sovereignty, and regulatory arbitrage. The BIS's warning is not accidental. Its decision to speak out at this juncture is precisely because the size of stablecoins has reached a truly systemic threshold. For reference: Australia's national foreign exchange reserves are approximately $150 billion, and Brazil's are approximately $145 billion. Two private companies hold more US Treasury bonds than the national reserves of most medium-sized economies. This means that the pace of stablecoin issuance is already substantially influencing the trend of short-term US Treasury yields—an area once exclusively controlled by the Federal Reserve.

    II. The GENIUS Act: The First Federal Law on Stablecoins in US History

    To understand the current situation, we must start from the source. On July 18, 2025, Trump officially signed the "Directing and Establishing the National Innovation for Stablecoins in the United States Act" (GENIUS Act). This is the first federal legislation in US history specifically targeting payment-type stablecoins, marking the formal transition of US crypto regulation from "fragmented enforcement" to "systematic governance".

      To understand the current situation, we must start from the source.

      However, just as the regulatory framework was being implemented at an accelerated pace, a debate surrounding whether stablecoins could pay interest nearly overturned the next stage of the entire legislative process—the CLARITY Act.

      The matter began with Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong. In January 2026, he publicly withdrew his support for the Senate Banking Committee's version of the CLARITY bill, citing the core reason that the Senate version explicitly prohibits stablecoin issuers and their affiliated distribution platforms from paying any form of passive income to holders. If enacted, this provision would directly cut off Coinbase's stablecoin interest business—the project generated $355 million in revenue for the company in the third quarter of 2025. Just hours after Armstrong withdrew his support, Senate Banking Committee Chairman Tim Scott announced a delay in the review process. Lobbying groups in the banking industry, such as the American Bankers Association (ABA), strongly advocated for this ban, arguing simply that if stablecoins could offer returns close to short-term US Treasury yields (historically 3.5%-5%), while bank deposit rates were near zero, it would trigger a trillion-dollar exodus of deposits, threatening the lending capacity of community banks. — Lobbying stance of the American Banking Association, early 2026. However, a report released by the White House Council of Economic Advisers (CEA) on April 8 directly refuted this logic: a complete ban on stablecoin yields would only increase bank lending by about $2.1 billion (less than 0.02%), while resulting in a net welfare loss of $800 million for consumers. Even under the most extreme assumptions, the boost to community bank lending would be negligible. On March 20, Senators Tillis and Alsobrooks proposed a compromise framework: "rewards" linked to user activity could be allowed, while purely passive savings yields would be prohibited. The problem is that there is still no clear precedent for the legal and technical boundaries between the two. This debate is essentially a game of bets by the banking industry, crypto-native institutions, regulators, and Congress in a redrawing of the boundaries of their interests. IV. The Four-Way Game: Who Wins, Who Loses, and Who's Watching? Winner: USDC (Circle) Leading in compliance, monthly audits, and a regulatory framework tailored to the GENIUS Act institutionalize its competitive advantage. It is expected to hold over 90% of the compliance market share. The adaptable party: USDT (Tether) Offshore operating models are under pressure, but with a circulating supply of $18.7 billion in a $320 billion market, it already surpasses the size of most sovereign reserves. Regulatory compliance will be its only ticket to enter the US market. The victims: Small and medium-sized crypto platforms High licensing costs and compliance barriers will exclude the vast majority of small and medium-sized stablecoin projects from the competition. This has actually deepened the competitive advantage of leading platforms like Coinbase. Strategic Observer: Hong Kong & Asian Markets Hong Kong's Stablecoin Ordinance was completed by the end of 2025, providing a compliant path for multi-currency (including offshore RMB) stablecoins. Whether the US legislative framework can force the acceleration of RMB stablecoin development is the most noteworthy variable.

        V. Dollar on the Blockchain: This is a "Re-dollarization" Strategy

        Stripping away the technological and legal shell, the political and economic logic of the GENIUS Act is actually extremely clear:It is a digital strategic counterattack by the United States against the wave of "de-dollarization".

        Over the past decade, the global trend of "de-dollarization" has continued to intensify: Russia and Iran have shifted to local currency settlements; BRICS countries are exploring alternative payment networks; and some Middle Eastern oil-producing countries have begun accepting non-dollar oil settlements. The US dollar's share of global foreign exchange reserves has declined from over 70% in the 2000s to approximately 59% today. Stablecoins offer a sophisticated countermeasure. Their underlying logic is a closed loop: users buy stablecoins → issuers use reserve funds to purchase US Treasury bonds → demand for US dollars expands, and US Treasury bonds find buyers. Circle's CEO has publicly stated that every $10 billion increase in USDC reserves will bring approximately $6 billion in short-term financing to the US Treasury. Standard Chartered estimates that the expansion of compliant stablecoins could generate up to $1.6 trillion in new demand for short-term Treasury bonds. More importantly, stablecoins bypass SWIFT and the traditional banking system, entering a gray area untouched by the US dollar—ordinary citizens in Argentina after the peso's sharp devaluation, daily transactions in Venezuela, and small merchants making cross-border payments in Southeast Asia… These people use USDT, meaning the on-chain reach of the US dollar extends further and deeper than any US bank. VI. Three Key Variables Still Unresolved The GENIUS Act has been passed, but the entire framework is still in a period of intensive rule-making. The following three variables will determine the final form of this "dollar on-chain" strategy: ① Whether the CLARITY Act will pass. This bill aims to establish a regulatory framework covering the entire digital asset market, and on March 17th, it completed the historic ruling by the SEC and CFTC formally recognizing major assets such as Bitcoin and Ethereum as "digital goods." The problem is: the November 2026 midterm elections constitute a hard deadline—if the House of Representatives flips, the pro-crypto Republican legislative coalition will collapse, and the bill could be stalled for up to four years. JPMorgan Chase predicts that if the CLARITY bill passes by mid-2026, institutional capital inflows into digital assets will accelerate significantly in the second half of the year. ② The crypto asset inclination of Federal Reserve Chair candidate Kevin Warsh. His financial statements show a crypto portfolio exceeding $100 million—if this candidate takes over the Federal Reserve, digital assets will be integrated into mainstream financial infrastructure policy considerations in an unprecedented way. ③ Asia's reaction speed. The BIS warning on April 20th specifically highlighted the risk of "digital dollarization" facing the Asian banking system: even if Asian countries issue their own stablecoins as a protective measure, these tokens can still be exchanged for USDT in a few clicks on decentralized exchanges after being put on the blockchain. The pace of development of Hong Kong's offshore RMB stablecoin and the competition between Singapore and Dubai's regulatory standards will constitute "Asian variables" in the future digital currency order. A significant point: the US spent three years blocking China's AI chips, while simultaneously spending another three years weaving the dollar into the global blockchain infrastructure. The former is defensive—preventing the opponent; the latter is offensive—proactive expansion. Two games are running in parallel. The brilliance of the stablecoin route lies in the fact that it doesn't require government intervention; private enterprises will spontaneously carry out the work of "dollar colonization"—every USDT transaction inadvertently reinforces the network effect of the dollar. The BIS is issuing warnings, Asian central banks are feeling anxious, and China's CIPS and digital yuan are accelerating—the real battle in this war over "whose currency will become the settlement unit in the on-chain world" has only just begun.

data.title: The Web3 Carnival was a resounding success, but why is the cryptocurrency world still "absent" from stablecoins?
data.description:
The Web3 Carnival just concluded successfully yesterday at the Hong Kong Convention and Exhibition Centre. Compared to the diverse array of new concepts and narratives—AI x Crypto, RWA, smart hardware, Ethereum applications, and innovative quantum computing upgrade solutions—a comment from the VDX CEO left a deeper impression on our team: "Every time I catch up with friends in the crypto world, there's nothing new to say; everything's happening between financial institutions now." In contrast to the Carnival's grand scale, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority's official issuance of the first batch of stablecoin issuer licenses on April 10th marks a new and highly standardized phase in the regulation of digital assets in Hong Kong and even Asia. Out of a total of thirty-six applications, only HSBC and Idolpoint Financial Technology Co., Ltd. ultimately stood out and were granted entry. A staggering 94% elimination rate clearly conveys the core principle of regulators: "stability first, zero tolerance for risk." This result is less a sign of free market competition and more a result of a meticulously designed institutional selection process—the weighting of financial innovation and risk control by Hong Kong regulators speaks for itself. Today, our team will discuss why the Web3 industry hasn't entered the stablecoin arena. Institutional Framework and Initial Results: The Regulatory Logic Behind a "Strict Selection" As we analyzed before, Hong Kong's stablecoin regulation follows the principle of "same activity, same risk, same regulation." Hong Kong Financial Secretary Paul Chan Mo-po also emphasized in his opening speech at the carnival that decentralization and digitalization do not mean lowering regulatory standards; on the contrary, they should promote more effective compliance and supervision to ensure that AI is predictable, traceable, and controllable by humans. A rigorous compliance project is being gradually built. The core is the Stablecoin Ordinance, which will come into effect in 2025. Specifically, the high regulatory threshold is reflected in several hard dimensions. First, there are extremely high capital and reserve requirements. Applicants must have a paid-in capital of no less than HK$25 million, and the issued stablecoin must be 100% backed by high-quality liquid assets such as cash, bank deposits, and short-term government bonds, with independent custody and monthly third-party audits. Second, there are zero-tolerance risk control and compliance standards. The review focuses heavily on anti-money laundering, counter-terrorism financing, customer asset protection, extreme market stress testing, and a redemption mechanism that ensures completion within one business day. Finally, regulators explicitly prohibit algorithmic stablecoin models, allowing only fiat-backed stablecoins, thus eliminating the risk of "de-anchoring" due to fluctuations in collateral value or flaws in mechanism design. Under these stringent selection criteria, the approval of HSBC and Dingdian Financial is no longer accidental, but an inevitable result of regulatory logic. Eddie Yue, Chief Executive of the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA), explicitly stated that the core consideration in the approval process is whether the applicant possesses "sufficient risk management capabilities and a concrete application plan." In other words, in issuing the first batch of licenses, regulators prioritized not the disruptive nature of the technology or the market's potential, but rather the institution's stability, risk controllability, and the close connection between its business and the real economy. This positioning directly elevates stablecoins from the category of "crypto asset speculation tools" to the level of "financial infrastructure." The core attributes of infrastructure are security, stability, and trustworthiness—precisely the core capital accumulated by traditional financial giants over a century of refinement. Winner Profile: Why Did "Banking Affiliates" Dominate the Start? Analyzing the backgrounds of the two licensed institutions reveals a common core characteristic: a strong traditional financial lineage and robust comprehensive credit backing. This is no coincidence, but rather a direct manifestation of regulatory intent. 1. HSBC Bank The Hongkong and Shanghai Banking Corporation Limited (HSBC), as one of Hong Kong's three note-issuing banks, is itself a crucial component of the Hong Kong dollar credit system. It boasts top-tier international credit ratings, a trillion-dollar balance sheet, a mature global cross-border payment and settlement network, and a proven anti-money laundering and risk management system. For regulators, having HSBC issue stablecoins is equivalent to directly grafting new products onto the most solid existing financial credit foundation, minimizing public concerns about the issuer's solvency. Its business plan is also highly pragmatic: the Hong Kong dollar stablecoin, planned for launch in the second half of 2026, will be seamlessly integrated into its PayMe payment wallet, which already has over 3.3 million users, and HSBC Hong Kong's mobile banking application. This means that stablecoins are not being cultivated from scratch to foster user habits, but rather are a functional extension of the existing vast retail financial ecosystem. Initially, it focuses on everyday scenarios such as peer-to-peer transfers and personal-to-merchant payments, achieving a closed-loop cycle of "issuance-circulation-application." This path, relying on the existing ecosystem and serving real-world transactions, has the lowest risk and the strongest controllability. 2. Idol Financial Technology Co., Ltd. represents another "hybrid advantage" model. Its shareholding structure is key to understanding its approval: Standard Chartered Bank (Hong Kong) Limited (50.5% controlling stake), Hong Kong Telecom, and Animoca Brands. This combination represents a fusion of "financial compliance + telecommunications channels + Web3 ecosystem." Standard Chartered Bank, as another note-issuing bank in Hong Kong, provides a bank-level compliance foundation, capital custody, and risk control capabilities on par with HSBC; Hong Kong Telecom, through its "Pay-to-Win" payment tools, brings a vast local offline merchant network and C-end user reach. Animoca Brands, as a well-known Web3 investment and development group, contributes its understanding and connectivity capabilities within the blockchain ecosystem. The "B2B2C" business model chosen by Dingdian, which reaches users through authorized distributors and focuses on tokenized real-world asset settlement and cross-border payment solutions, indicates that its strategy is not to directly compete with HSBC in the red ocean of retail payments, but rather to build a financial settlement layer serving institutions and specific ecosystems. Regulatory approval of this architecture can be seen as a cautious tolerance for scenario innovation and ecosystem cooperation while adhering to the bottom line of "bank-led" development. The success of these two institutions points to a common conclusion: In the initial stage of stablecoin development, Hong Kong regulators prioritized "credit" and "controllability" over "innovation" and "openness." The long-term accumulated capital strength, compliance culture, regulatory communication channels, and public trust of the banking system constitute a moat that cannot be surpassed by technological advantages in the short term. This essentially confines the issuance of stablecoins to the core of the existing financial power structure. The Web3 "Table" Dilemma: Compliance Gap and Policy Constraints In stark contrast to the successful "table entry" of two traditional financial institutions, the collective absence of numerous Web3 native companies and tech giants is striking. Ant Group, JD Technology, and HashKey, among other previously high-profile applicants, did not appear in the initial list. This result directly reflects the systemic dilemma faced by the Web3 industry under the current stringent Hong Kong regulatory framework. The primary dilemma lies in the structural gap in compliance capabilities. The "decentralization," "anonymity," and "open finance" often touted in the Web3 concept fundamentally conflict with current regulatory requirements. The Stablecoin Regulations require strict customer identification and due diligence comparable to banks, making it virtually impossible for DeFi applications relying on anonymous wallets or aiming to reduce intermediaries to operate compliantly under the current framework. Regulators require stablecoin issuers to have robust transaction monitoring systems, clear fund custody chains, and redemption guarantees to cope with runs. These represent extremely high operational and cost barriers for many Web3 startups with flat organizational structures and novel asset custody methods. Regulators are scrutinizing auditable, traceable, and accountable centralized governance capabilities, which are precisely what many Web3 projects intentionally downplay or technically circumvent. Secondly, the transmission effect of mainland policies constitutes an invisible barrier. According to public reports, mainland regulators, out of consideration for maintaining monetary sovereignty and financial stability, have adopted a cautious attitude towards domestic companies participating in overseas stablecoin businesses, and have even provided "window guidance." This constitutes an insurmountable policy constraint for technology companies whose shareholder background or business is closely related to the mainland market. Although Hong Kong enjoys a unique financial status under the "one country, two systems" principle, it must fully consider the overall financial security and regulatory direction of the country on major issues involving cross-border capital flows and potential risks of currency substitution. Therefore, even if these technology companies possess strength in technology, capital, and application scenarios, their applications may be hindered because they cannot fully demonstrate their compatibility with mainland regulatory policies. At a deeper level, this is a clash between development path and regulation. The Web3 industry originated from a grassroots innovation culture of "code is law" and "rapid iteration and willingness to try and fail." Financial regulation, especially regulation involving monetary payment functions, is philosophically based on the principles of "law above code" and "risk prevention and rights protection." Hong Kong's top-down approach of "establishing strict laws first, then issuing licenses," unlike the more lenient "sandbox-first" model in places like Singapore, means that only those "plugs" that can fully adapt to the existing regulatory "sockets" can be connected. For many Web3 companies, their business models themselves represent a challenge to the old "socket" standards. Transforming themselves to fully comply with stringent bank-level standards is not only extremely costly but may even undermine their core innovative value. Therefore, the failure of the Web3 industry to be among the first to "play the game" is not due to a single factor, but rather a comprehensive result of the interplay of compliance capabilities, the overall policy environment, and development methodologies. This demonstrates that in the field of stablecoins, considered digital financial infrastructure, pure "technological advantages" or "ecological vitality" are insufficient to open the regulatory door. A deep understanding, respect for, and integration into the traditional financial regulatory system are the scarce resources at this stage. The impact on ordinary people: convenience and boundaries coexist. The implementation of compliant stablecoins will gradually but tangibly change the financial lives of ordinary Hong Kong residents and even mainland investors interested in this market. Its impact is specific and subtle, while its boundaries are clearly defined. The positive impact is mainly reflected in the improvement of payment efficiency and financial accessibility. For Hong Kong residents, stablecoins first and foremost provide a new digital payment option. HSBC's integration of stablecoins into PayMe's scenario planning means that users may be able to achieve instant, low-cost peer-to-peer transfers based on stablecoins, as well as direct payments to participating merchants in the future. This is not intended to replace existing FPS or e-wallets, but rather to provide an additional, potentially more convenient option, especially when interacting with on-chain assets. For individuals and small merchants with cross-border needs, stablecoins can theoretically provide a faster and lower-cost cross-border payment solution than traditional wire transfers. Standard Chartered's example of shortening cross-border settlement from "T+3" to near real-time reflects this vision. However, ordinary people must be aware of the boundaries and risks involved. First, stablecoins themselves are not deposits and do not generate interest; their value depends entirely on the issuer's sufficient, high-quality reserve assets as collateral and their own creditworthiness. Despite regulation, "compliance" does not equate to "zero risk." Custodian risk and liquidity risk under extreme market conditions still exist. Secondly, the expansion of application scenarios will be gradual and controlled. Regulators explicitly prohibit over-the-counter (OTC) exchanges from offering stablecoins to the public, significantly limiting their ease of offline circulation. In the short term, the main applications of stablecoins will be limited to the ecosystem of licensed institutions or specific scenarios in which they cooperate, making it difficult to circulate freely like cash or ordinary deposits. Finally, regarding investment functions, stablecoins are merely pricing and settlement tools. Investing in tokenized assets with them still carries the risk of market volatility in the underlying assets; it is by no means a guaranteed profit. Binance Co-CEO He Yi also advises industry practitioners to allocate to leading assets and adhere to the principle of rational investment. The Question of "Purchasing" by Mainland Residents: Double Separation of Law and Policy A highly concerned question is: Can mainland residents directly purchase and hold these compliant stablecoins issued in Hong Kong? Analysis from the current legal and policy framework shows that the answer is no, and there are multiple layers of separation.

First, the foreign exchange management system constitutes the first line of defense.

 According to Article 17 of the "Regulations of the People's Republic of China on Foreign Exchange Control," domestic institutions and individuals engaging in the issuance and trading of overseas securities must register as required, and if approval or filing with the competent authority is required, it must be completed before foreign exchange registration. While compliant stablecoins are not traditional securities, as financial instruments issued overseas and denominated in Hong Kong dollars, their trading involves the exchange of RMB and HKD and cross-border capital flows, naturally falling under the scope of foreign exchange control. Currently, there are no compliant foreign exchange and cross-border remittance channels for mainland residents to directly purchase overseas stablecoins. Normal cross-border remittances made by individuals through the banking system are subject to clear purpose review, and are generally not permitted for the purchase of virtual currencies or related products. Secondly, **mainland China's regulatory policies on virtual currencies and related businesses are clear and strict.** Mainland regulatory authorities have repeatedly stated that virtual currency-related business activities are illegal financial activities. Although Hong Kong's stablecoins are defined as "compliant," their technological essence is still based on blockchain, and under the mainland regulatory context, their connection with virtual currencies is difficult to sever. Domestic financial institutions and payment institutions are not allowed to provide services for any virtual currency transactions. Therefore, attempting to directly purchase stablecoins from licensed Hong Kong institutions through mainland bank accounts will trigger anti-money laundering monitoring and compliance interception at the operational level. Furthermore, the compliance requirements of Hong Kong licensed institutions themselves will also block proactive sales to mainland residents. According to Article 18 of the "Pilot Measures for the Settlement of RMB for Overseas Direct Investment," banks must fulfill anti-money laundering obligations and assess destination risks when handling relevant cross-border business. For institutions like Dingdian Financial, their CEO has clearly stated that they will only conduct business in a well-regulated and compliant ecosystem. Given the clear regulatory stance of the mainland, any licensed institution that proactively promotes or sells stablecoins to mainland residents will face significant compliance risks, potentially even affecting its license status. Therefore, they will inevitably establish strict geofencing and customer identification mechanisms to exclude mainland residents from their service scope. So, are mainland investors completely unable to participate? The answer is no, but the path is indirect. As the material states, the market provides channels to indirectly share in the industry's growth dividends by investing in related brokerage stocks, fintech ETFs, and other securities products. This falls under the category of securities investment activities conducted within mainland China or through legal channels such as the Stock Connect program, and is legally completely different from directly holding and trading stablecoins. For mainland residents, understanding and adhering to this boundary is crucial. Any attempt to purchase stablecoins across borders through underground channels or disguised trade not only poses no guarantee of fund security but may also violate relevant national foreign exchange management and anti-money laundering laws and regulations, facing the dual risks of financial loss and legal sanctions. In conclusion, the issuance of the first batch of stablecoin licenses in Hong Kong establishes a starting model dominated by traditional finance with absolutely controllable risks. The victory of HSBC and IDP represents the regulators' ultimate vote on the integration of credit, capital, and real-world scenarios. The temporary absence of Web3 forces highlights the profound gap that still needs to be bridged between innovative passion and regulatory rationality in the field of financial infrastructure. For ordinary people, stablecoins will bring limited but tangible convenience, along with the essential lesson of understanding the risks of new financial products. For mainland residents, clear legal boundaries have been drawn; holding stablecoins is not illegal, but direct purchase may face obstacles. As for when Web3 will truly "take the table," perhaps when the next carnival opens and the first batch of licensed institutions' stablecoins are officially launched, the regulatory framework will undergo another round of adjustments. Like everyone else, the Sa Jie team is waiting to see.

data.title: Meta And Microsoft Trim Nearly 17,000 Jobs Combined As AI Investment Reaches Hundreds Of Billions
data.description:
AI Spending Surge Triggers Fresh Job Cuts Across Silicon Valley

Job cuts are spreading again across major US technology firms as companies redirect billions into artificial intelligence infrastructure, reshaping workforce plans while chasing efficiency gains.

Meta and Microsoft are the latest to confirm significant changes, with both firms linking decisions to rising AI-related costs and long-term restructuring.

Layoff news this morning...

- #Meta cutting 8000 jobs...closing 6000 open roles

- #Microsoft giving first ever voluntary retirement

- #KPMG axing 10% audit partners

- #Nike cutting 1400 jobs. pic.twitter.com/5f5ESpD6Ox

— Anisha Jain (@_anishaj) April 24, 2026

AI Spending Pressure Drives Fresh Workforce Cuts Across Big Tech

The latest wave of reductions comes as leading tech groups continue pouring money into data centres, AI models and computing infrastructure.

Industry-wide capital spending among Amazon, Google, Meta and Microsoft is projected to reach around US$650 billion in 2026, reflecting the scale of investment required to support AI systems.

At the same time, several firms have already trimmed headcount over the past year, including Amazon, Oracle, Snap and Block, as post-pandemic hiring expansions are reversed.

Microsoft shares slipped about 4% on Thursday and are down roughly 15% this year, while Meta has remained broadly flat.

Why Meta Is Cutting 8,000 Jobs While Expanding AI Bets

Meta has confirmed it will reduce its workforce by around 10%, affecting approximately 8,000 employees, while also cancelling plans to fill roughly 6,000 open roles.

The cuts are scheduled to begin on 20 May 2026.

We originally picked META up on our tracker March 13th.

8,000 is only half of the 16,000 they will be laying off this year. https://t.co/ccWEgMktanpic.twitter.com/Z29MAPWsL1

— Official Layoff (@LayoffAI) April 23, 2026

The company said the move is part of efforts to manage costs while continuing heavy investment in AI.

Meta’s spending on artificial intelligence is expected to reach US$135 billion this year, roughly matching what it spent over the previous three years combined.

In an internal memo, chief people officer Janelle Gale said:

“We’re doing this as part of our continued effort to run the company more efficiently and to allow us to offset the other investments we’re making.”

She added:

“This is not an easy trade-off and it will mean letting go of people who have made meaningful contributions to Meta during their time here.”

Here is the letter Meta Platforms $META just sent to employees about the cutting laying off 10% of the workforce:

"Over the last few weeks we have been working on some changes to our organization that will result in us laying off around 10% of the company on May 20, and closing… pic.twitter.com/7gXZcD0TA4

— Evan (@StockMKTNewz) April 23, 2026

Meta has already carried out smaller rounds of layoffs this year and continues to shift resources towards AI development, including tools designed to improve advertising performance and new hardware projects such as smart glasses.

Chief executive Mark Zuckerberg has also signalled the direction of travel, saying:

“I think that 2026 is going to be the year that AI starts to dramatically change the way that we work.”

Microsoft Offers Buyouts To Senior Staff As Costs Come Under Review

Microsoft is also adjusting its workforce strategy, offering voluntary buyouts to a portion of its US employees in a first-of-its-kind programme for the company.

Around 7% of US staff at senior director level or below may be eligible, with one condition requiring that age plus years of service add up to at least 70.

The programme could cover roughly 8,750 employees, according to people familiar with the plan.

SILENT LAYOFF ALERT: MICROSOFT OFFERING VOLUNTARY RETIREMENT 🚨

Up to 8,750 US employees eligible (~7% of US workforce). Age + years of service must total 70 or more.

Meanwhile, our H-1B tool shows 83,171 LCA filings for foreign labor since 2015. pic.twitter.com/bWYdI98RKX

— Official Layoff (@LayoffAI) April 23, 2026

The move comes after Microsoft previously cut thousands of jobs in 2025, as it continues heavy investment in AI and cloud infrastructure, including its Azure platform and Copilot tools.

A memo from Microsoft’s chief people officer Amy Coleman described the scheme as giving eligible workers “the choice to take that next step on their own terms, with generous company support.”

Are Efficiency Gains From AI Reshaping Tech Employment?

Executives across the sector are increasingly linking restructuring decisions to productivity gains from AI systems.

Companies argue that advanced tools now allow fewer staff to complete work that previously required larger teams.

Zuckerberg has pointed to this shift directly, noting that AI-enabled workers can now handle tasks that once required entire groups, reinforcing a push towards leaner organisational structures.

Meta to layoff 8000 employees, 10% of its workforce!!!

Mark Zuckerberg: "We will get to a point where all the code in our apps and the AI it generates will also be written by AI engineers instead of people engineers."

It's a living proof that NO CAPITALIST EVER CARES FOR ITS… pic.twitter.com/ZtTQoiVRzp

— Swapna Kumar Panda (@swapnakpanda) April 20, 2026

At the same time, Big Tech firms continue to expand AI investment even as they reduce headcount, highlighting a growing divide between rising infrastructure costs and shrinking operational teams.

The result is a labour market in flux, where cost control, automation and large-scale AI development are increasingly shaping hiring decisions across the industry.

A running list of layoffs across corporate America so far in 2026:

• Snap Inc. $SNAP: ~1,000 jobs cut (16% of workforce) + 300 open roles eliminated

• The Walt Disney Company $DIS: ~1,000 layoffs, mainly in marketing

• Meta Platforms $META: Potentially 20%+ workforce cuts +…

— Dividendology (@dividendology) April 23, 2026

data.title: US Military Turns Bitcoin Into a Cyber Weapon, Runs Live Node for ‘Power Projection
data.description:
From Financial Asset To Military Weapon

A senior US military official has confirmed that the United States is now operating inside the Bitcoin network itself, marking a symbolic shift for a protocol originally designed to exist beyond state control.

JUST IN: 🇺🇸 Four-star military officer Admiral Samuel Paparo confirms the USA is running a Bitcoin node.

"We have a node on the Bitcoin network right now. We're doing a number of operational tests to secure and protect networks using the Bitcoin protocol." pic.twitter.com/4JIOIMtlTW

— Bitcoin Magazine (@BitcoinMagazine) April 22, 2026

Speaking before Congress, Samuel Paparo, commander of the United States Indo-Pacific Command, disclosed that the military is running a live Bitcoin node as part of ongoing cybersecurity and network research, and that the government isn't mining Bitcoin or accumulating it as an asset.

This is the first time Bitcoin has been framed not as a monetary instrument, but as a "military weapon". By operating a node, the US gains direct, unfiltered access to the blockchain’s data layer—allowing it to observe network activity, test cryptographic models, and study the resilience of decentralized systems in real time.

US Military just confirmed they’re running a live Bitcoin node for cybersecurity testing.

Admiral Paparo told Congress it’s not about mining or holding BTC — they’re using the protocol as a straight cryptographic + proof-of-work tool to monitor networks and project power.… pic.twitter.com/8BbEbEoxkv

— Merben Baterna (@SERRBEN72) April 23, 2026

This new discovery is showcasing the versatility of digital assets. Apart from just the financial and regulatory lens, the military is treating the protocol as a form of digital infrastructure, one that can be analyzed, stress-tested and potentially repurposed for securing networks or countering adversarial cyber capabilities.

A State Actor Inside A Decentralized System

Technically, a single node carries no influence over Bitcoin’s consensus, which is maintained by a globally distributed network of participants. Yet the optics are harder to ignore. A system built to minimize reliance on governments now includes direct participation from one.

This does not undermine Bitcoin’s decentralization, but it highlights how deeply the protocol has penetrated global systems. Governments are no longer just regulating or observing from the outside—they are beginning to interact with the network at an infrastructure level.

Paparo linked the military’s interest in Bitcoin to broader objectives around cybersecurity and what he described as the ability to “project power” through technological systems. In parallel, he pointed to US support for stablecoin frameworks as part of a wider strategy to reinforce dollar dominance in the digital economy.

🎥 WATCH: Admiral Samuel Paparo highlights growing military interest in Bitcoin: pic.twitter.com/6ifdXcJetL

— CoinDesk (@CoinDesk) April 22, 2026

Taken together, the approach suggests a dual-track strategy: while Bitcoin is studied as a decentralized security model, state-backed digital assets are positioned as instruments of financial influence.

The disclosure leaves key details of the military’s research classified, but it signals a clear direction. Bitcoin is no longer just a disruptive financial experiment—it is increasingly being examined as a component of 21st-century cyber infrastructure.

data.title: After the AI ​​voted, the only winner was USDC.
data.description:
Author:Clow，Plain Language Blockchain

The market has been chasing AI coins for three years, but the one that is truly used by AI may not have been in most people's minds from the beginning. The most counterintuitive thing is that FET, TAO, RENDER, and other coins with "AI" in their names don't actually use the AI ​​agents running on the network themselves.

A true AI agent running on the network doesn't need FET to buy GPU computing power, doesn't need TAO to call APIs, and doesn't need RENDER to pay royalties to the model. An AI Agent can process hundreds of payments per second, making it impossible for credit cards to withstand the 2.9% plus $0.30 fixed fee; AI Agents require sub-second settlement, while ACH wire transfers are still calculated in three-day increments; AI Agents don't look at the screen or click buttons, rendering all payment confirmation mechanisms that require human intervention in the "confirm payment" process ineffective. All things claiming to be AI tokens are absent from the true AI economy. Yet, money is still flowing wildly between machines, amounting to hundreds of millions of dollars daily. What is flowing? What exactly is the AI ​​token? It's not FET, not TAO, and not RENDER. It's USDC. A status code that's been hidden for thirty years suddenly awakens. In 2025, Coinbase, in conjunction with Circle and Google, did something very strange. They unearthed something that had been hidden for thirty years. HTTP 402. There are several status codes in the HTTP protocol that everyone sees every day: 200... A 404 error means the request wasn't found, and a 500 error means the server crashed. But the 402 error, since the protocol was drafted in the 1990s, remained unused, officially called Payment Required. The drafters reserved it, intending for a native payment mechanism to be needed on the internet someday. For a full thirty years, no one used it. It wasn't until the x402 protocol in 2025 that it was activated for the first time. The meaning is simple: When an AI Agent accesses a paid API, the old method involved registering an account, obtaining an API Key, linking a credit card, and a human confirming. Now, it's different. The agent sends a request, and the server directly returns a 402. The status code contains payment metadata, clearly stating the amount, receiving address, and accepting blockchain. The agent's wallet sees a 402, automatically signs a USDC transfer, resends the request, and completes the process within two seconds. There is no account, no API key, and no human confirmation. Jeremy Allaire gave a figure: In the next three to five years, there will be billions of AI agents running on the internet, issuing payments 24/7. This number sounds exaggerated, but if you accept the premise that machines work thousands of times more frequently than humans, it immediately becomes very reasonable. A technical staff member might sign 10 contracts a day, while an agent might sign 10 contracts in a second. After the x402 protocol is activated, USDC's status changes. It's no longer just a "stablecoin," no longer something for cryptocurrency traders to trade pairs. It's become a protocol primitive for the machine internet, placed on the same level as TCP/IP and HTTP. Simply put, the internet has transformed from a network for "information transfer between people" to a network for "value transfer between machines." And the universal currency used for this transfer is USDC. 98% of the answers have already been cast. Do you think this is just a narrative? The data has already voted. Circle's 2026 data: Over 98% of agent-driven payments chose USDC. Not 60%, not 80%, but 98%. The average payment is $0.31, an amount a human would almost never initiate alone, since even a cup of coffee at Starbucks starts at $4. Such a small transaction can only be the work of machines. Circle created its own blockchain called Arc, specifically for stablecoin finance. The cost per transaction on the Arc chain is not the previously cited $0.00001, but approximately $0.01. US dollars. What truly reduces USDC transfers to the $0.00001 level is not the single gas transaction on the Arc chain, but rather the aggregation off-chain followed by unified on-chain settlement. For comparison: Credit cards charge 2.9% plus 30 cents, while bank wire transfers are $15 to $50. The fixed fees for traditional payments are an order of magnitude higher than the total amount of a single machine payment. For an AI agent to send a $0.31 payment through a traditional banking system, the transaction fees alone would be nearly 100 times its own cost. This isn't "friction," it's "infeasibility." The real problem stablecoin tracks solve isn't about pushing every on-chain cost infinitely close to zero, but rather making machine-level micro-payments feasible for the first time through programmable settlement and batch aggregation. What about those truly called AI tokens? FET, TAO, and RENDER are basically invisible in an AI Agent's wallet. They lie dormant in speculators' contract accounts, fluctuating by 5% to 10% daily. If an AI Agent uses TAO to pay for computing power rental, it might be able to pay 1000 hours today, only 900 tomorrow, and 1100 the day after. Hours. It can't do budgeting, can't do financial planning, and can't sign any price-anchored contracts with external suppliers. What's even more ironic? Those cryptocurrencies bearing the name "AI" don't circulate at all in the real AI economy. Their purpose is for human speculation, not for machines. A "stablecoin," on the contrary, has become the lifeblood of machine civilization. TAO is electricity, USDC is cash. Some might say, then FET and TAO aren't completely useless. That's true, they have their scenarios. But this scenario is never "currency," but "commodity." HashKey, in a Web3 report, provides a more precise dichotomy: AI tokens are the smallest semantic unit of computing power consumption, similar to electricity and gas; blockchain tokens are the smallest programmatic unit of value flow, which is cash. Let me translate. TAO is like the electricity flowing from your home outlet, USDC is like the cash in your wallet. You use TAO to train models, allocate computing power, and run inference, just like you use electricity to light up your room and boil water. But you wouldn't take a kilowatt-hour of electricity to the supermarket to pay; you would use cash. An AI agent might use TAO to coordinate computational tasks within its internal network. But as soon as it goes out into the world—renting AWS servers, ordering plush toys from Amazon, or paying a human writer—it only accepts USDC. Because AWS doesn't accept TAO, Amazon doesn't accept FET, and human workers certainly don't take a renter's fee as their salary. The two sides of the balance sheet are completely different things. In late 2024, Stripe acquired Bridge, a stablecoin infrastructure company, for $1.1 billion. This figure didn't attract much attention at the time, but in retrospect, it signaled the surrender of traditional payment giants to machine payments. A company that had relied on credit card fees for two decades bought a ticket to the machine economy for $1.1 billion. Then VanEck made a prediction: By 2027, AI agent-driven on-chain automated transactions will reach $5 billion per day, with a compound annual growth rate exceeding 120%. $5 billion a day equals $1.8 trillion annually. What does this number mean? Currently, the daily volume of global cross-border payments via SWIFT is approximately $5 to $6 trillion. This means that in three years, automatic transfers between AI agents could account for one-third of global cross-border payments. And almost all of this $1.8 trillion will go through USDC. Summary: The market has misrepresented the story of AI tokens. The real AI tokens are not FET, TAO, or RENDER; they are speculative tools betting on the future appreciation of some AI infrastructure. The true AI token is USDC, which is the settlement currency used daily by AI Agents in real money. One is a narrative, the other is a water pipe. One is for telling stories to humans, the other is for machines to run production. How does the pendulum move? In 2022, everyone thought anything labeled "AI" was valuable; in 2024, everyone thought it was a bubble; and in 2026, the real winner was discovered to be a stablecoin. Wall Street's pricing logic is always slower than technological reality, and this time is no exception. The internet didn't kill the real economy; the real economy learned e-commerce. Cryptocurrencies didn't overthrow the dollar; the dollar learned to put itself on the blockchain. AI didn't create a new currency; AI chose the oldest one, the dollar, and then used its programmatic version. Is that "AI token" you bought actually being used by AI? A true AI token doesn't need the name "AI".

data.title: A Comprehensive Guide to GPT-5.5: Starting Today, OpenAI Will No Longer Sell Tokens
data.description:
Author: Li Hailun, Tencent Technology

On April 23 local time, OpenAI officially released its new generation flagship model GPT-5.5, which it positions as "a new level of intelligence for real-world work," and is also an important step towards a new way of computing.

84.9% of tasks reach professional level

Figure: Comparison of GPT-5.5 with competing products in core benchmark tests such as Terminal-Bench 2.0, GDPval, and OSWorld-Verified

First, let's look at the performance of the evaluation model in real professional scenarios.

OpenAI used a benchmark test called "GDPval," which requires models to complete a full set of professional tasks. The test covers 44 professional scenarios, including financial modeling, legal analysis, data science reporting, operational planning, and more. The results show that GPT-5.5 reached or exceeded the level of industry professionals in 84.9% of the tasks. In comparison, GPT-5.4 scored 83.0%, Claude Opus 4.7 80.3%, and Gemini 3.1 Pro only 67.3%. This gap isn't limited to the overall score. In the spreadsheet modeling task, GPT-5.5 achieved 88.5% in internal testing; it also outperformed its predecessor in investment banking-level modeling tasks. Early testers' feedback was consistent: GPT-5.5 Pro's responses showed significant improvements over GPT-5.4 Pro in terms of comprehensiveness, structure, and practicality, particularly in the business, legal, education, and data science fields. Looking at numbers alone can be monotonous, so OpenAI went all out this time, revealing its own workstations. OpenAI stated that over 85% of its employees use Codex weekly, covering multiple departments including finance, communications, marketing, product, and data science. The communications team used it to analyze six months of speech invitation data, building an automated grading process; the finance team used it to review 24,771 K-1 tax forms, totaling 71,637 pages, completing the task two weeks ahead of schedule compared to last year; and the marketing team saved 5 to 10 hours per person per week by automating weekly report generation. This isn't just a lab demo; it's become part of daily work. OpenAI claims that GPT-5.5 is currently its most powerful autonomous programming model. On Terminal-Bench 2.0 (testing complex command-line workflows requiring planning, iteration, and tool coordination), GPT-5.5 scored 82.7%, an improvement of nearly 8 percentage points compared to GPT-5.4's 75.1%, while consuming fewer tokens. On SWE-Bench Pro (evaluating the ability to solve real-world GitHub problems on the first try), GPT-5.5 scored 58.6%. In the internal Expert-SWE benchmark (a long-cycle programming task with a median human completion time of approximately 20 hours), GPT-5.5 also outperformed GPT-5.4.

Figure: Scatter plot of Terminal-Bench 2.0 and Expert-SWE

Driven by GPT-5.5, Codex can now independently complete the entire development process from code generation, functional testing to visual debugging, starting from a single prompt.

The official OpenAI demonstration cases show that the space mission application is built based on real NASA orbital data, supports 3D interactive control, and achieves realistic physical accuracy in orbital mechanics simulation; the earthquake tracker connects to real-time data sources and completes visualization, demonstrating that the model has the complete ability to call external APIs, process dynamic data, and render in real time. Regarding user feedback, Every founder and CEO Dan Shipper shared an experience: He previously encountered a bug after deployment, which he couldn't fix himself for several days, and finally had to ask the company's best engineer to rewrite part of the system. After GPT-5.5 was released, he conducted an experiment—putting the model back to the state before the bug was fixed, to see if it could automatically arrive at the same solution as the engineer. GPT-5.4 couldn't do it, but GPT-5.5 could. He commented: "This is the first programming model I've used that truly has conceptual clarity."

An NVIDIA engineer's comment was even more blunt: "Losing access to GPT-5.5 feels like amputation."

Cursor co-founder and CEO Michael Truell added: GPT-5.5 is smarter and more resilient than GPT-5.4, able to persist longer in complex, long-duration tasks without prematurely stopping—which is exactly what engineering work needs most. Knowledge Work: For the First Time, AI Can Truly "Use" a Computer. In the OSWorld-Verified test (which tests whether a model can independently operate a real computer environment), GPT-5.5 achieved a success rate of 78.7%, higher than GPT-5.4's 75.0% and also better than Claude Opus 4.7's 78.0%. This isn't just screenshot analysis; it's real screen control: seeing the interface, clicking, inputting, switching between multiple tools, until the task is completed. GPT-5.5 gives people the first experience that AI can truly share the same computer with you. In the Tau2-bench benchmark for telecom customer service workflows, GPT-5.5 achieved an accuracy of 98.0% without prompt word optimization, while GPT-5.4 only achieved 92.8%. This means the model has a sufficiently deep understanding of the task intent to handle complex multi-step dialogue processes without the need for carefully designed prompt words. Regarding tool search capabilities, GPT-5.5 scored 84.4% in the BrowseComp test, while GPT-5.5 Pro achieved an even higher 90.1%, indicating that in research-type tasks requiring comprehensive reasoning across multiple information sources, the model demonstrated a strong ability for continuous retrieval and information integration. Scientific Research: Assisting in the Discovery of New Mathematical Proofs In this release, GPT-5.5's performance in the scientific research field is perhaps the most unexpected aspect. In the past, when we talked about AI in scientific research, it was more of an "auxiliary tool" used for literature review, coding, and data processing. But this time, its role has clearly shifted forward, participating in more core aspects: complex reasoning, and even the discovery itself. On GeneBench (a multi-stage data analysis benchmark in genetics and quantitative biology), GPT-5.5 scored 25.0%, while GPT-5.4 scored 19.0%. These tasks typically correspond to several days' worth of work for scientific experts. The models need to reason about potentially erroneous data, handle hidden confounding factors, and correctly implement modern statistical methods under almost no supervision. As can be seen from the graph, as the number of output tokens increases, GPT-5.5 consistently outperforms GPT-5.4 in terms of score improvement, with a significant gap appearing around 15,000 tokens—meaning that for long tasks requiring deep reasoning, GPT-5.5's advantage further amplifies with increasing task complexity. On BixBench (a real-world benchmark for bioinformatics and data analytics), GPT-5.5 outperformed GPT-5.4's 74.0% with a score of 80.5%, ranking among the top-scoring models. What truly garnered attention was a specific case: an internal version of GPT-5.5 equipped with a custom tool framework helped discover a new mathematical proof about Ramsey numbers, which was then verified in the formal proof tool Lean. Ramsey numbers are a core research object in combinatorics, and achievements in this field are extremely rare and technically challenging. This wasn't AI providing code or explanations, but a genuine contribution of a mathematical proof. Its practical applications are equally compelling. Derya Unutmaz, professor of immunology at Jackson Lab, used GPT-5.5 Pro to analyze a gene expression dataset containing 62 samples and nearly 28,000 genes, generating a detailed research report and extracting key findings and research questions—a task he said typically takes teams several months to complete. Bartosz Naskręcki, assistant professor of mathematics at Poznan Adam Mickiewicz University, built an algebraic geometry application in 11 minutes using GPT-5.5 in Codex, based solely on a single prompt. This application visualized the intersection of two quadratic surfaces and transformed the resulting curve into a Weierstrass model. The equation coefficients displayed in real-time on the right can be directly used for subsequent mathematical research; the entire process, from the prompt to a working research tool, was completed independently by the model. Image: Screenshot of an algebraic geometry application built by Professor Bartosz Naskręcki—visualization of quadric surface intersections and real-time calculation interface for the Weierstrass equation. Axiom Bio co-founder Brandon White's assessment is even more direct: "If OpenAI maintains this momentum, the foundation of drug discovery will change by the end of the year." Inference Efficiency: AI Optimizes Its Infrastructure for the First Time This release has an easily overlooked detail, but it may be the most noteworthy technical advancement. GPT-5.5 is a larger and more powerful model, yet its single-token latency in real-world service is on par with GPT-5.4. To maintain the same latency with greater capabilities, OpenAI redesigned the inference system as a whole—and Codex and GPT-5.5 themselves were directly involved in the optimization process. This is visually apparent from the Artificial Analysis intelligence index graph: the horizontal axis represents the total output tokens (logarithmic scale), and the vertical axis represents the overall intelligence score. GPT-5.5's curve not only outperforms GPT-5.4, Claude Opus 4.7, and Gemini 3.1 Pro Preview in all aspects of scoring, but more importantly, it achieves the same score level that other models need to consume more tokens to reach in the range where token consumption is low—stronger capabilities at lower costs, which is a direct manifestation of "efficiency improvement".

Figure: Artificial Analysis Smart Index Line Chart

Specifically, the team faced the problem of load balancing: previously, requests were split into a fixed number of blocks to balance the workload on the GPUs, but static block splitting was not optimal for all traffic patterns. Codex analyzed several weeks of production traffic data and wrote a custom heuristic algorithm, which improved token generation speed by more than 20%. GPT-5.5 is co-designed, co-trained, and co-deployed with NVIDIA GB200 and GB300 NVL72 systems. In other words, this generation of models participates in optimizing the inference architecture of the service itself—this is not a metaphor, but a literal statement that "AI improves the system running itself." Cybersecurity: Enhanced Capabilities, Tighter Controls GPT-5.5 shows a clear improvement in cybersecurity capabilities. In CyberGym testing, GPT-5.5 scored 81.8%, GPT-5.4 scored 79.0%, and Claude Opus 4.7 scored 73.1%. In the internal Capture the Flag (CTF) challenge, GPT-5.5 scored 88.1%, and GPT-5.4 scored 83.7%. OpenAI has rated GPT-5.5's cybersecurity and biological/chemical capabilities as "High" under the Contingency Preparedness Framework, not yet reaching the "Critical" level, but representing a clear improvement over its predecessor. At the same time, it acknowledges that the newly deployed, more stringent risk classifier "may initially be inconvenient for some users" and will continue to be adjusted. To balance defense needs with access restrictions, OpenAI launched the "Trusted Access to Cybersecurity" program: eligible security researchers and critical infrastructure defenders can apply for more lenient access permissions to use advanced cybersecurity capabilities with less friction. The logic behind this is that capability proliferation is an irreversible trend, and a more realistic path than restricting proliferation is to allow defenders to use the most powerful tools before attackers.

data.title: DeFi's core strengths are destroying themselves.
data.description:
Author: Thejaswini, Source: Buttercup Network, Translated by: Shaw Jinse Finance

“Death” is a very heavy word. As I've always maintained, I'm optimistic about DeFi. But then again, every few months, DeFi repeats the same risky farce, then pretends to be caught off guard by the outcome. Over time, one has to admire this persistence.

On April 18th, attackers exploited a vulnerability in Kelp DAO's LayerZero cross-chain bridge to steal 116,500 rsETH, worth approximately $292 million, representing about 18% of the token's total circulating supply. The attackers didn't immediately sell them off—hackers are now more cautious. They deposited 89,567 of the stolen tokens into Aave V3 as collateral, using this to borrow native ETH, and then made their escape. Thus, Aave incurred approximately $196 million in bad debt, and these tokens used as collateral should not have appeared out of thin air just hours earlier. Within 48 hours, Aave's total value locked (TVL) plummeted from $26.4 billion to nearly $20 billion, and has now fallen to $14 billion; its native token, AAVE, has also dropped by 16%. SparkLend and Fluid have frozen the rsETH-related lending market, Lido has suspended its earnETH product, and Ethena, even without direct risk exposure, has urgently shut down all its LayerZero cross-chain bridges. Like a neighbor's house on fire, an uncle, fearing the spread, throws all his furniture out the window. In the current industry climate, how can I criticize such self-preservation? Aave founder Stani Kulechov clarified on Twitter that the Aave contract itself was not compromised, the emergency pause mechanism functioned correctly, and the entire system operated exactly as designed. If this system truly operates as designed, then the design itself is a disaster. Kelp's cross-chain bridges adopt a single-node decentralized verification network (1-of-1 DVN) architecture. Below is an explanation of its specific meaning: Single Validator: Only one entity is responsible for verifying transactions. Single Signature Authorization: Asset transfers can be approved with just a digital "confirmation signature." Unlimited Authority: With just this one signature, one has the ultimate authority to mint hundreds of millions of dollars worth of new tokens. Traditional banks, even for a simple $10,000 wire transfer, typically require approval and signatures from two to three managers. However, the Kelp protocol's cross-chain bridge, which handles billions of dollars in cross-chain asset transfers, prioritizes low cost and a "simple" architecture, completely lacking effective checks and balances and risk control mechanisms. Next, let's talk about Aave. Aave previously accepted rsETH as collateral, with a loan-to-value (LTV) ratio as high as 93%. Simply put, for every $1 worth of rsETH a user deposits, Aave lends out $0.93 of real assets. Only with extremely high confidence in the collateral would such aggressive parameters be set. However, the total circulating supply of this token, which Aave trusts, can increase by 18% with just one transaction. The deposit limit for this token is extremely lenient; a single wallet can deposit 90,000 rsETH at once without triggering any risk control warnings. How can such risk parameters be described? Perhaps the answer is self-evident. Every post-mortem review yields the same conclusion: the smart contract executed flawlessly, the oracle data reporting was normal, the liquidation mechanism was triggered according to the rules, and the emergency pause function was successfully activated within minutes. These are all facts, but none of them grasp the core of the problem. Aave's protocol design is fundamentally flawed: as long as the risk hasn't been publicly exposed, the system allows anonymous wallets to deposit hundreds of millions of dollars in collateralized assets without underlying backing, and then issue real lending debt based on this. This design stems from a fundamental belief: as long as assets have oracle pricing and the deposit limit is sufficiently generous, they can be used unconditionally as collateral for lending. Those who believe in "code is law" often take this as reassurance. However, "code is law" doesn't mean the rules are reasonable; it only means the rules will be mechanically enforced without any room for maneuver. If the rules state, "Any token with oracle pricing can be used for high-value collateralized lending," then this unconditional enforcement is the most frightening aspect. For the past six years, DeFi has been touting composability. Everyone's talking about "financial Lego": nested protocols, free flow of funds, and highly efficient and sophisticated mechanisms. In contrast, traditional banks would never readily accept interbank IOUs as collateral; they always have dedicated risk control teams conducting thorough reviews. This is a lesson learned from the 2008 financial crisis: highly interconnected financial positions only lead to shared risk, not shared returns. Globally, all financial regulatory agencies saw the fatal flaws of DeFi architecture as early as 2019. Layered counterparty risk, zero-discount risk control, no limits on transaction amounts, and a lack of a lender of last resort—yet the entire industry blindly believed that on-chain transparency could replace all risk control measures. Aave's core liquidity pool utilization once reached 100%, preventing depositors from redeeming their Ethereum. In traditional finance, this would be a classic bank run. When users deposit assets into Aave, they are essentially endorsing four layers of risk: Aave's risk control parameters, the rsETH oracle, the Kelp cross-chain bridge, and Kelp's decentralized verification network configuration. The last one is something most Aave users have never even heard of. The Cost Bill: Kelp incident losses: $292 million; Drift incident losses three weeks ago: $285 million; In just 21 days, these two industry-leading protocols have collectively lost nearly $500 million. In the first quarter of 2026, the number of crypto venture capital deals plummeted by 48.9% year-on-year, and the number of funding rounds shrank from 358 to 183. During the same quarter, AI companies raised a staggering $242 billion, accounting for approximately 80% of the total global venture capital investment. Capital that was initially intended to improve the underlying infrastructure of DeFi is now flowing into other more speculative and attractive areas. The innovations DeFi truly desperately needs—a conservative and prudent loan-to-value ratio, a multi-validator cross-chain bridge architecture, a robust insurance guarantee mechanism, and routine audits of basic, standard configurations—have been ignored. No one has ever become rich by considering the potential disasters of a single validator node (DVN) becoming a single point of failure. Of course, the hackers of the April 18th attack were the sole exception. We can do some simple calculations: On April 18th, Aave's security reserve pool held $390 million, while the platform's outstanding loan balance reached a staggering $17.82 billion. This implied capital adequacy ratio was only 2.2%. Basel III requires banks to have a minimum capital adequacy ratio of 8%, with JPMorgan Chase actually meeting the requirement at 15.5%. Aave's staking penalty cap for AAVE is 30%, meaning the maximum usable funds in this safety net are only about $180 million, further reducing the effective capital adequacy ratio to around 1.0%. A simple and feasible solution has always existed: **adopting a multi-validator (Multi-DVN) architecture, lowering the loan-to-value ratio for assets with questionable creditworthiness, and setting a truly effective cap on token supply.** The most costly solution, however, is to acknowledge that **composability is never an advantageous feature; it's merely a beautifully packaged risk transmission mechanism.** I don't believe this is simply a security issue. For six years, DeFi has been trapped in a misconception: equating on-chain transparency with asset security. And every 18 months, this cognitive fallacy is repeatedly confirmed by larger-scale incidents. "Financial Lego" sounds wonderful. But it accurately describes the current reality: stacking plastic blocks on a burning table. This is a lesson I learned from my elders, and it's incredibly insightful.

data.title: DeepSeek V4 Preview: Entering an Era of Millions of Contextual Accessible Resources
data.description:
Source: DeepSeek Official WeChat Account

Today, the preview version of DeepSeek's new model series, DeepSeek-V4, was officially launched and simultaneously open-sourced.

DeepSeek-V4 boasts a million-word ultra-long context, achieving leading performance in both domestic and open-source fields in Agent capabilities, world knowledge, and inference capabilities.

Source: DeepSeek Official WeChat Account

Today, the preview version of DeepSeek-V4, a brand-new model series from DeepSeek, was officially launched and simultaneously open-sourced.

DeepSeek-V4 features a million-word ultra-long context, achieving leading performance in Agent capabilities, world knowledge, and inference performance both domestically and in the open-source field.

The model is available in two versions based on size:

Starting today, log in to the official website  chat.deepseek.com  or the official App to interact with the latest DeepSeek-V4 and explore the new experience of 1M ultra-long context memory. The API service has been updated accordingly; you can call it by changing model_name to deepseek-v4-pro or deepseek-v4-flash.

...

DeepSeek-V4-Pro: Performance Comparable to Top Closed-Source Models

  • Significantly Improved Agent Capabilities: Compared to its predecessor, DeepSeek-V4-Pro's agent capabilities are significantly enhanced. In Agentic Coding benchmarks, V4-Pro has reached the best performance among current open-source models and also performs excellently in other agent-related benchmarks. Currently, DeepSeek-V4 has become the agentic coding model used by company employees. According to evaluation feedback, its user experience is superior to Sonnet 4.5, and its delivery quality is close to Opus 4.6's non-thinking mode, but it still lags behind Opus 4.6's thinking mode. Rich World Knowledge: In world knowledge evaluations, DeepSeek-V4-Pro significantly outperforms other open-source models, only slightly lagging behind the top closed-source model Gemini-Pro-3.1. World-Class Inference Performance: In evaluations of mathematics, STEM, and competition-style code, DeepSeek-V4-Pro surpasses all currently publicly evaluated open-source models, achieving excellent results comparable to the world's top closed-source models.

DeepSeek-V4-Flash: A Faster, More Efficient, and Economical Choice

  • Compared to DeepSeek-V4-Pro, DeepSeek-V4-Flash is slightly inferior in terms of world knowledge reserves, but it demonstrates similar inference capabilities. Due to its smaller model parameters and activations, V4-Flash offers a faster and more economical API service.

DeepSeek-V4-Flash: A Faster, More Efficient, and Economical Choice

  • Compared to DeepSeek-V4-Pro, DeepSeek-V4-Flash is slightly inferior in terms of world knowledge reserves, but it demonstrates similar inference capabilities. Furthermore, due to its smaller model parameters and activations, V4-Flash provides a faster and more economical API service.

Computational cost and memory capacity of DeepSeek-V4 and DeepSeek-V3.2 vary with context length

Agent-specific optimization

DeepSeek-V4 for Claude Code ... It has been adapted and optimized for mainstream Agent products such as OpenClaw, OpenCode, and CodeBuddy, resulting in improved performance in code tasks and document generation tasks. The image below shows an example of a PPT slide generated by V4-Pro within a certain Agent framework:

API Access

Currently, the DeepSeek API has been simultaneously launched on V4-Pro and V4-Flash, supporting OpenAI.

The ChatCompletions and Anthropic interfaces. When accessing a new model, the base_url remains the same, but the model parameter needs to be changed to deepseek-v4-pro or deepseek-v4-flash. The maximum context size for both V4-Pro and V4-Flash is 1MB. Both support both non-thinking mode and thinking mode. The thinking mode allows setting the thinking intensity (high/max) using the `reasoning_effort` parameter. For complex Agent scenarios, it is recommended to use thinking mode and set the intensity to max. For model invocation and parameter adjustment methods, please refer to the API documentation: https://api-docs.deepseek.com/zh-cn/guides/thinking_mode. Please note: The two model names deepseek-chat and deepseek-reasoner in the old API will be discontinued in three months (July 24, 2026). At this stage, these two model names refer to the non-thinking mode and thinking mode of deepseek-v4-flash, respectively.

Open source weighting and local deployment

  • DeepSeek-V4 Model open source link:

https://huggingface.co/collections/deepseek-ai/deepseek-v4

https://modelscope.cn/collections/deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-V4

data.title: Tesla Records $173M Loss From Its Bitcoin Investment After BTC Falls To $78,000
data.description:
Tesla Loses Millions In Bitcoin, But Keeps Its Long-Term Bitcoin Position Intact

Tesla has reported a $173 million loss from its bitcoin investment as of the first quarter of 2026. Yet the company keeps its crypto strategy unchanged....

Quick take: Tesla ate a $173M after tax impairment loss (accounting hit from BTC price drop $114k to $88k) on digital assets.

Zero sales. Holdings steady at 11,509 BTC.

SpaceX (Elon led) bags 8,285 BTC too. Signals corporate diamond hands: BTC as reserve asset > short term… pic.twitter.com/Zk1dGkJIIB

— JAVLIS - Your Trading #AIAgent (@javliscom) April 23, 2026

The automaker confirmed it continues to hold 11,509 BTC, signaling an unchanged long-term strategy despite renewed volatility triggered by the rising geopolitical unheavel in the middle east.

However, the reported loss only showed a flawed side of the story, stemming from the accounting requirements that force companies to only record losses without allowing immediate recognition of potential gains in cases of price increase.

This asymmetry means Tesla’s financial results can show significant downside during market dips, while failing to capture potential upside as prices rebound—creating a disconnect between reported earnings and actual exposure.

Despite the paper loss, Tesla’s unchanged holdings reinforce its position among a small group of public companies maintaining Bitcoin as a treasury asset. The company’s exposure, valued in the hundreds of millions of dollars, continues to fluctuate with market conditions but reflects a strategy focused on long-term participation rather than short-term trading.

🚨 STRATEGY OVERTAKES BLACKROCK AS LARGEST BITCOIN HOLDER

Saylor’s Strategy has officially surpassed BlackRock’s IBIT ETF, now holding ~815,061 BTC vs ~802,824 BTC for $IBIT.

The flip comes after its latest buy of 34,164 $BTC for ~$2.54B last week, its third-largest purchase… pic.twitter.com/Iy828mJYAv

— Coin Bureau (@coinbureau) April 21, 2026

The approach mirrors a broader trend among crypto-aligned corporates that absorb interim volatility while maintaining conviction in Bitcoin’s role as a store of value.

Corporate Crypto Strategies Face Structural Constraints

Tesla’s latest results highlight a persistent challenge for companies holding digital assets: accounting frameworks have struggled to keep pace with the characteristics of cryptocurrencies.

Purpose-built for autonomy

Cybercab in production now at Giga Texas pic.twitter.com/Y9qG3KyWBa

— Tesla (@Tesla) April 23, 2026

The current treatment can amplify earnings volatility and obscure underlying performance, particularly during periods of sharp price movement. As more firms consider adding Bitcoin to their balance sheets, the limitations of these standards are becoming increasingly visible.

For Tesla, the $173 million loss underscores how market swings—and the rules governing their reporting—can shape financial outcomes without altering the company’s actual position in crypto.

data.title: Intel's Q1 earnings report delivered a resounding slap in the face to Wall Street.
data.description:
Author: xiaopi, Wall Street Insights

After the market closed on April 23, Intel's stock price rose 20%, approaching $80, a new high since the dot-com bubble.

But this increase itself is actually more worthy of consideration than the financial report figures. Prior to this, among the 34 analysts on Wall Street who tracked Intel, 24 gave it a "Hold" rating, with an average target price of $55.33.

... At that time, Intel's stock price was already at 66—meaning that most institutions' judgments were not just conservative, but had fallen behind reality. A quarterly report that significantly exceeded expectations not only boosted the stock price but also exposed this discrepancy. This 20% after-hours surge was, to some extent, the market completing a belated pricing correction for the institutions. Exceeding Expectations Across the Board: Revenue 13.6 billion, analysts expected 12.4 billion, exceeding expectations by approximately 9.3%. Adjusted EPS 0.29, expected 0.01. Q2 revenue guidance midpoint 14.3 billion, expected 13.1 billion. All three core indicators significantly exceeded expectations, and the exceedance was not marginal but systemic—the implied volatility bet in the options market before the release was 9.3%, but it actually increased by more than two times, meaning that even hedging institutions were caught off guard. The net loss under GAAP was 3.7 billion, and the difference of approximately 4 billion between non-GAAP and GAAP figures came from stock-based compensation, depreciation, and restructuring expenses. These are the real costs Intel is incurring in its foundry transformation, not accounting noise, but also not new information to the market—analysts have already factored them in. What are the 24 Hold institutions waiting for? Behind the Hold consensus, it's not primarily a valuation issue. In the past two years, as Intel has continuously lost its process technology leadership and its market share has been eroded by AMD, Wall Street's deepest concern about the company has been its execution: Lip-Bu Tan's story sounds good, but Intel has said "this time it really will be good" too many times. Therefore, even if the demand logic for AI agents is clear and the story of the CPU's value revaluation at the inference layer holds true, most institutions are still waiting for concrete proof, rather than betting on narratives. This financial report provides precisely that proof—in the form of contracts. Intel announced it will manufacture chips for Elon Musk's Terafab factory, with customers including SpaceX, xAI, and Tesla; on the same day, it signed a multi-year contract with Google, under which Xeon CPUs will provide computing power for AI inference and other workloads on Google Cloud. The timing of these two contracts, announced on the same day as the quarterly report release, is not a coincidence, but rather management's way of telling the market in black and white: "Our demand isn't just a trend prediction; it's already secured revenue." The confidence in the Q2 guidance also stems from this—$13.8 billion to $14.8 billion, the midpoint of which is $1.2 billion higher than the consensus, representing a commitment to existing orders.
