The logic of data centers has changed. Data center and AI business revenue reached $5.1 billion, a year-over-year increase of 22%, with an operating profit margin of 31%. Before 2023, this division was considered the remnant of NVIDIA's dominance, but the large-scale deployment of AI agent architectures has changed the demand structure. Large model training uses GPUs, but inference—especially enterprise-oriented, latency-sensitive, medium-sized inference workloads—has seen a significant underestimation of CPU demand. As cloud vendors face fierce competition for GPU resources, they have begun to procure server CPUs in large quantities to handle these tasks, and Intel's Xeon is currently the most reliable option in terms of supply. Lip-Bu Tan mentioned in the conference call that several customers are "actively evaluating" the next-generation 18A process technology. The weight of this wording—not just "interested," but "actively evaluating"—indicates that the commercialization of the foundry business is moving from the intention stage to the substantive stage. In contrast, client computing revenue reached $7.7 billion, a year-over-year increase of 1%. The concept of "AI PCs" has been discussed for two years, and Intel has released the Core Ultra series, but this replacement cycle has not yet brought about a real demand surge. This business segment contributes more than half of the company's revenue, yet it has seen almost no growth—making it the least attractive part of this financial report and one of the most real risks. Once the AI ​​demand cycle in data centers fluctuates, CCG has almost no buffering capacity. Foundry Services: How Long Is the Road Ahead? Intel Foundry Services revenue was $5.4 billion, up $165.4 billion year-over-year and $162.4 billion year-over-year, only narrowing by $72 million compared to the previous quarter. This rate of narrowing is usually described as "progress in line with expectations" in investment bank analyst reports, but if you calculate it—at this pace, it will take years, not quarters, for IFS to reach break-even. One of the core reasons BNP Paribas upgraded its rating before the earnings report was a more optimistic data assessment of the 14A node (the successor to 18A)—if 18A mass production goes smoothly, the customer appeal of 14A will be further enhanced. However, every milestone on this path is a reason for holding institutions to wait and see, and something that true bulls must continuously verify. The ensuing chain reaction: The 24 holding institutions are now in a somewhat awkward position. The average target price is 55.33, the stock price touched 55.33 in after-hours trading, and the stock price touched 80 in after-hours trading. This gap can no longer be maintained by "remaining cautious"—when the stock price exceeds the target price by more than 40%, analysts either revise their judgment or admit that they have missed the boat. Historically, whenever such concentrated institutional lag pricing occurs, subsequent batch target price increases themselves act as a boost to the market: each new research report release becomes a bullish signal reported by the media. HSBC has already given a street-high target price of $95 before its earnings report; if more institutions follow suit with upward adjustments, this figure may become a new market anchor. Two developments deserve continued monitoring. First, the pace of the 18A process from "active evaluation" to "contract signing" and then to "mass production"—every specific development on this timeline will directly affect the speed at which IFS's losses narrow and will also affect Intel's foundry attractiveness to external customers. Secondly, the repositioning of those 24 holding institutions is not because their judgment determines the stock price trend, but because their collective adjustment provides an observable signal—when institutional consensus shifts from "primarily holding" to "primarily buying," it usually means that the market's perception of a company has undergone a fundamental change. This financial report doesn't change Intel's quarterly performance, but rather the question itself. For the past two years, the question has been "Can Intel survive?" After this financial report, that question has essentially been answered; the new question has become—how well can it survive, and how quickly?
